title: Comprehensive Guide Regression R INFS3822
body:
INFS3822 Comprehensive Guide in R including all steps required
             to build ML Regression Models



This guide summarises all the steps you learned during weeks 3 and 4 including data loading, pre-
processing (data cleaning and transformation including the conversion of categorical variables into
dummy variables), feature selection (using Pair Plot and Correlation Matrix Heatmap), splitting
data into training and testing datasets, model training and testing to assess the performance of the
models. This guide including the steps requires for building ML models for regression problems
using linear regression, decision tree and neural network in R.
First, upload the file data_online_retail_customers.csv into this notebook on Google Colab.
Once the dataset is uploaded, you can conduct the following steps.


1    Loading Libraries
The following libraries are the ones used during weeks 3 and 4 that cover the required libraries for
logistic regression, decision tree, and neural networks.
# install all R packages needed
install . packages ( " gridExtra " )
install . packages ( " dplyr " )
install . packages ( " corrplot " )
install . packages ( " rpart . plot " )
install . packages ( " keras " )
install . packages ( " DALEX " )



library ( ggplot2 )
library ( dplyr )
library ( gridExtra )
library ( corrplot )
library ( rpart )
library ( rpart . plot )
library ( keras )
library ( DALEX )




2    Loading the Data from a CSV File
# Load the data
data <- read . csv ( " data _ online _ retail _ customers . csv " )




                                                    1
 3    Data Cleaning
# Check for missing values
missing _ values <- is . na ( data )
print ( sum ( missing _ values ) )
[1] 0
# Remove rows with missing values , if any
data <- na . omit ( data )




4    Data Transformation
# Check the data type of all variables
summary ( data )

# Convert categorical columns ( Class : character ) into ' factor ' type ( i . e . category
    )
data $ Gender = as . factor ( data $ Gender )
data $ Promotion _ Response = as . factor ( data $ Promotion _ Response )




                                             2
 Further data transformations
Uncomment the lines you need by removing the # Modify the code to fit your needs
# In this dataset , the target variable is Churned , and it is of type ' logical ' ( i .
    e . boolean ) .
# If the target variable is binary ( e . g . Yes / No , Good / Bad , Success / Fail ) , make
    sure you convert it into boolean variable as follows :
# data $ Churned = data $ Churned == 'Yes '

#   Convert date columns into datetime based on your objectives .
#   For further details , refer to Week4 _ Pre _ Seminar _ Using _ R _ for _ Clustering
#   data [ ' vdate '] = pd . to _ datetime ( data [ ' vdate ']) # for dates in the format m / d / yyyy
#   data [ ' vdate '] = pd . to _ datetime ( data [ ' vdate '] , dayfirst = True ) # for dates in the
      format d / m / yyyy

# data $ vdate <- as . Date ( data $ vdate , format = "% m / % d / % Y ") # for dates in the format
     m / d / yyyy
# data $ vdate <- as . Date ( data $ vdate , format = "% d / % m / % Y ") # for dates in the format
     d / m / yyyy

# If you have a variable representing a date , you can transform it into a
     categorical variable ( Think of what would be the categories that are relevant
     to your analysis objectives )
# For further details , refer to Week4 _ Pre _ Seminar _ Using _ R _ for _ Clustering
# Create a categorical variable for seasons
# get _ season <- function ( date ) {
# month <- month ( date )
# if ( month % in % c (12 , 1 , 2) ) {
#      return ( ' Winter ')
# } else if ( month % in % c (3 , 4 , 5) ) {
#      return ( ' Spring ')
# } else if ( month % in % c (6 , 7 , 8) ) {
#      return ( ' Summer ')
# } else {
#      return ( ' Autumn ')
# }
# }
#
# data $ season <- sapply ( data $ vdate , get _ season )
# data $ season <- as . factor ( data $ season )

# You can transform some data values . For example , convert '5+ ' to numeric 5 in
    the ' rcount ' column ( Week4 _ Pre _ Seminar _ Using _ R _ for _ Clustering )
# data [ which ( data $ rcount == "5+") ,] $ rcount = "5"
# data $ rcount <- as . integer ( data $ rcount )




# Scale the numeric input data
# Identify numeric columns
numeric _ columns <- sapply ( data , is . numeric )

# Scale numeric columns
data [ , numeric _ columns ] <- scale ( data [ , numeric _ columns ])




                                                   3
 5     Feature Selection
# Split the data into inputs ( X as features ) and outputs ( y as target variable ) .
# Initial feature selection ( i . e . selecting which variables should be included in
    X ) is required here as follows .
# You can exclude variables that represent unique identifiers because they don ' t
    have any impact on the target variable .
# You can exclude variables that are text descriptions and not useful as
    categories .
# You can exclude categorical variables that have too many possible values , as
    these would generate too many dummy variables . A model with too many variables
    cannot perform well and sometimes cause the machine to run out of memory . This
    would also help you focus on the most important variables .
X <- data _ selected <- data [ , ! ( names ( data ) % in % c ( " Customer _ ID " , " Total _ Spend " ) ) ]
y <- data _ selected <- data $ Total _ Spend




Pair Plot and Correlation Matrix Heatmap
# Determine the type of the target variable
target _ is _ numeric <- is . numeric ( y )
target _ is _ categorical <- is . factor ( y )
target _ is _ logical <- is . logical ( y )

target <- " Total _ Spend "

# Plot each feature against the target variable using appropriate plots in a grid
num _ features <- ncol ( X )
num _ cols <- 3
num _ rows <- ceiling ( num _ features / num _ cols ) # Ceiling division

# Create an empty list to store plots
plots <- list ()

# Plot each feature against the target variable
for ( i in seq _ len ( num _ features ) ) {
  feature <- colnames ( X ) [ i ]
  feature _ is _ numeric <- is . numeric ( X [[ feature ]])
  feature _ is _ categorical <- is . factor ( X [[ feature ]])

    # Select appropriate plot
    if (( target _ is _ categorical || target _ is _ logical ) & & feature _ is _ numeric ) {
      p <- ggplot ( data . frame (X , y ) , aes _ string ( x = " y " , y = feature ) ) +
         geom _ boxplot () +
         labs ( title = paste ( " Box Plot of " , feature , " by " , target ) , x = target , y =
      feature )
    } else if ( target _ is _ numeric & & feature _ is _ numeric ) {
      p <- ggplot ( data . frame (X , y ) , aes _ string ( x = feature , y = " y " ) ) +
         geom _ point () +
         labs ( title = paste ( " Scatter Plot of " , feature , " vs " , target ) , x = feature , y
       = target )
    } else if (( target _ is _ categorical || target _ is _ logical ) & & feature _ is _
      categorical ) {
      p <- ggplot ( data . frame (X , y ) , aes _ string ( x = feature , fill = " y " ) ) +
         geom _ bar ( position = " dodge " ) +
         labs ( title = paste ( " Count Plot of " , feature , " by " , target ) , x = feature ,
      fill = target )
    } else if ( target _ is _ numeric & & feature _ is _ categorical ) {


                                                     4
       p <- ggplot ( data . frame (X , y ) , aes _ string ( x = feature , y = " y " ) ) +
         geom _ boxplot () +
         labs ( title = paste ( " Box Plot of " , target , " by " , feature ) , x = feature , y =
      target )
    } else {
      p <- NULL
    }

    if ( ! is . null ( p ) ) {
      plots [[ i ]] <- p
    }
}

# Arrange plots in a grid
do . call ( grid . arrange , c ( plots , ncol = num _ cols ) )




                                                    5
 # Convert categorical variables into dummy variables to include individual
    categories as variables . Please make sure that you do this before constructing
    the correlation heatmap . This would help you identify the most important
    features to be included in your models . It is also important to do this before
    training the models .
X <- as . data . frame ( model . matrix ( ~ . , data = X ) ) [ , -1]

# Let ' s plot the correlation matrix heatmap to check which variables are important
     ( i . e . correlated with the target variable ) .
# Combine X and y into one data frame
data2 <- cbind (X , data . frame ( Total _ Spend = y ) )

# Convert non - numeric columns to numeric ( if applicable )
data2 <- data . frame ( lapply ( data2 , function ( x ) {
   if ( is . factor ( x ) ) as . numeric ( as . factor ( x ) )
   else if ( is . character ( x ) ) as . numeric ( as . factor ( x ) )
   else x
}) )

# Compute the correlation matrix
corr _ matrix <- cor ( data2 )

# Plot the heatmap
corrplot ( corr _ matrix , method = " color " , tl . col = " black " , tl . cex = 0.8 , number . cex
      = 0.7 , addCoef . col = " black " , col = colorRampPalette ( c ( " blue " , " white " , " red " )
    ) (200) , title = " Correlation Matrix Heatmap " , mar = c (0 , 0 , 1 , 0) )




                                                   6
 # Further feature selection based on the results of pair plots and / or correlation
    matrix heatmap .
# You can exclude variables that are not correlated with the targe variable .
# When 2 variables ( other than the target variable ) are highly correlated to each
    other , you can remove one of them to address multi - colinearity problem ( keep
    the one that is highly correlated with the target variable ) .
X <- data _ selected <- X [ , ! ( names ( X ) % in % c ( " Satisfaction _ Score " , " Promotion _
    Response _ Responded " ) ) ]




6    Split the data into training and testing sets
# Split the data into training and testing sets
set . seed (123) # For reproducibility
n <- nrow ( X )
splitIndex <- sample (1: n , size = round (0.8 * n ) , replace = FALSE )
X _ train <- X [ splitIndex ,]
y _ train <- y [ splitIndex ]
X _ test <- X [ - splitIndex ,]
y _ test <- y [ - splitIndex ]




7    Train and test a Linear Regression Model
# Train a linear regression model on the training data
model <- lm ( y _ train ~ . , data = X _ train )

# Make predictions on the testing data
predictions <- predict ( model , newdata = X _ test )

# Calculate Mean Absolute Error ( MAE )
mae <- mean ( abs ( predictions - y _ test ) )

# Calculate Root Mean Squared Error ( RMSE )
rmse <- sqrt ( mean (( predictions - y _ test ) ^2) )

# Calculate R - squared
r _ squared <- 1 - ( sum (( predictions - y _ test ) ^2) / sum (( mean ( y _ train ) - y _ test ) ^2) )

# Print evaluation metrics
cat ( " Mean Absolute Error ( MAE ) : " , mae , " \ n " )
cat ( " Root Mean Squared Error ( RMSE ) : " , rmse , " \ n " )
cat ( "R - squared : " , r _ squared , " \ n " )

# Plot actual vs predicted values
plot ( y _ test , predictions , xlab = " Actual Total _ Spend " , ylab = " Predicted Total _
    Spend " , main = " Actual vs Predicted Total _ Spend " )
abline (0 , 1)

# Display feature coefficients
summary ( model )

“Answers to Week 3 Hands-On Exercise 2 Linear Regression - R” provided on Moodle.


8    Train a Decision Tree Regression Model
# Train a decision tree model on the training data with a continuous target
    variable
model <- rpart ( y _ train ~ . , data = X _ train , method = " anova " )

# Make predictions on the testing data
y _ pred <- predict ( model , newdata = X _ test )

# Calculate regression metrics
# Calculate Mean Squared Error ( MSE )
mse <- mean (( predictions - y _ test ) ^2)


                                                9
 # Calculate Mean Absolute Error ( MAE )
mae <- mean ( abs ( predictions - y _ test ) )
# Calculate R - squared
r2 <- 1 - ( sum (( y _ test - predictions ) ^2) / sum (( y _ test - mean ( y _ test ) ) ^2) )

message ( " Mean Squared Error : " , mse )
message ( " Mean Absolute Error : " , mae )
message ( "R - squared : " , r2 )

# Plot the decision tree
rpart . plot ( model )

# Display the decision tree structure in text form
message ( " Tree Structure : " )
print ( model )

# Display the feature importance scores
importance <- model $ variable . importance
message ( " Feature Importance Scores : " )
print ( importance )

                                              11
 9    Train a Neural Network Model
# Define the model
model <- keras _ model _ sequential () % >%
  layer _ dense ( units = 12 , input _ shape = ncol ( X _ train ) , activation = ' relu ') % >%
  layer _ dense ( units = 6 , activation = ' relu ') % >%
  layer _ dense ( units = 1 , activation = ' linear ')

# Compile the model
model % >% compile ( loss = ' mean _ squared _ error ' ,
  optimizer = ' adam ' , metrics = c ( ' mean _ absolute _ error ') )

# Train the model
model % >% fit (
  as . matrix ( X _ train ) , y _ train ,
  epochs = 5 , batch _ size = 200)

# Evaluate the model
score <- model % >% evaluate ( as . matrix ( X _ test ) , y _ test , verbose = 0)
score [ " mean _ absolute _ error " ]

# Make predictions with the model
predictions <- model % >% predict ( as . matrix ( X _ test ) )

# Print summary of predictions
summary ( predictions )

# Calculate performance metrics : Mean Absolute Error , Mean Squared Error , and R -
    squared
mae <- mean ( abs ( predictions - y _ test ) )
mse <- mean (( predictions - y _ test ) ^2)
rsq <- 1 - ( sum (( y _ test - predictions ) ^2) / sum (( y _ test - mean ( y _ test ) ) ^2) )

cat ( ' Mean Absolute Error : ' , mae , '\ n ')
cat ( ' Mean Squared Error : ' , mse , '\ n ')
cat ( 'R - squared : ' , rsq , '\ n ')

# Evaluate feature importance using Permutation Importance
# Define a prediction function for DALEX
predict _ function <- function ( model , newdata ) {
  as . numeric ( predict ( model , as . matrix ( newdata ) ) )
}

# Create an explainer object for DALEX
explainer <- DALEX :: explain (
  model ,
  data = as . data . frame ( X _ test ) ,
  y = as . numeric ( y _ test ) ,
  predict _ function = predict _ function ,
  label = " Neural Network "
)

# Calculate feature importances
importance <- DALEX :: model _ parts ( explainer )

# Print feature importances
print ( importance )
